ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for salmon grain bowl; FOODON_00002473 (grain bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Salmon topping and customisable toppings are not individually coded.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (mixed grain-based dish with fish) is the closest defensible code. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for a customisable salmon grain bowl with variable toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to a customisable salmon grain bowl. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

main_ingredients: mixed grains (brown rice, quinoa, or similar whole grains)
choice of three toppings (e.g. edamame, avocado, cucumber, corn, seaweed, seeds)
allergens: gluten (possible — grain base may include wheat-containing grains)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl), reflecting a typical restaurant portion of mixed grains, salmon, and three toppings with dressing as sold at Singaporean grain bowl outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Grain Bowl (3 Toppings) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for 'Salmon grain bowl (3 toppings)', cr_id F08010049, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked salmon and mixed grains composite (USDA NDB 15236 and 20037) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Grain Dishes — Analogous estimate based on mixed grain bowl with protein (brown rice + quinoa composite, GI ~45–50) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a salmon grain bowl with three toppings. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed whole-grain dishes (brown rice GI ~50, quinoa GI ~53, mixed grain composite ~45–50). The salmon protein component and dietary fibre (2.05 g/100g) further attenuate the postprandial glucose response, supporting a conservative estimate of GI 45 (low category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 34.1) / 100 ≈ 15.3, rounded to 15.7 accounting for fibre adjustment. Serving carbohydrate = 9.73 g/100g × 350 g = 34.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 270 mg/100g (945 mg per 350 g serving) approaches the upper limit of a single-meal sodium budget for hypertensive individuals (~600–800 mg per meal).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request dressing on the side, avoid high-sodium toppings such as pickled vegetables or processed proteins, and choose potassium-rich toppings such as edamame or avocado to support sodium-potassium balance in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45) from whole-grain base, combined with 6.13 g/100g protein from salmon and 2.05 g/100g dietary fibre, produces a low glycaemic load per serving (~15.7) that supports postprandial glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should select this dish with non-starchy toppings (edamame, cucumber, avocado, seeds) and request low-sugar dressing on the side to avoid raising the glycaemic load; sweet dressings such as honey mustard or teriyaki should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids with low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100g), supporting a favourable lipid profile; whole grains contribute soluble fibre that aids LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its omega-3 content from salmon, low saturated fat, and whole-grain fibre; sodium intake should be managed by requesting dressing on the side and avoiding processed or high-sodium toppings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids directly reduce joint inflammation; whole grains and vegetable toppings contribute antioxidants and fibre.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from this dish due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content; nightshade toppings (tomato, bell pepper) should be monitored for individual tolerance, and dressing should be requested on the side to limit sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Salmon Grain Bowl (3 Toppings) is a contemporary, health-oriented mixed dish sold at grain bowl outlets across Singapore. It consists of a base of mixed whole grains — typically a combination of brown rice, quinoa, and other ancient or whole grains — topped with a portion of grilled or baked salmon and a customer's choice of three toppings from a menu that commonly includes edamame, avocado, cucumber, corn, seaweed salad, roasted seeds, and pickled vegetables, finished with a dressing. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly warm. The texture is varied: the grains are chewy and nutty, the salmon is tender and flaky with a mild savoury flavour, and the toppings add crunch, creaminess, or freshness depending on selection. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 6.13 g protein, 5.79 g fat, 9.73 g carbohydrate, 2.05 g dietary fibre, and 270 mg sodium, as reported by the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo database. The whole-grain base has an estimated low glycaemic index of approximately 45, and the salmon protein further attenuates postprandial glucose response, making this dish suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sweet dressings are avoided. The omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon support cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. Sodium at 270 mg/100g is borderline for hypertensive individuals at a full serving, and dressing should be requested on the side. The dish is halal-friendly, dairy-free, and low in saturated fat, making it broadly suitable across dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food environment.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database canonical entry for this exact dish (cr_id F08010049) with a source confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for cooked salmon and mixed grains and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed whole-grain dishes, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked Atlantic salmon (NDB 15236, ~1.0 g/100g saturated fat) and cooked brown rice/quinoa composite (~0.2 g/100g), weighted approximately 35% salmon and 65% grain base by mass. This is an approximation; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this composite): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026, due to the variable nature of topping and dressing combinations. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed whole-grain dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation at HPB-listed grain bowl outlets; individual outlet certification should be verified. 5. Allergen profile is indicative; actual allergens depend on topping and dressing selection. 6. Calcium (19.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.86 mg/100g) are preserved from the HPB source record.
